local_names.romanized_local: Zhá yú liǔ hànbǎo (Chinese), Burger Fillet Ikan Goreng Tepung (Malay), Meen Fillet Burger (Tamil), Fried Fish Fillet Burger (Hindi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002764 (fish sandwich) is the closest available class. The input dish is a battered dory fillet in a ciabatta or brioche bun with tartar sauce and salad; no exact FoodOn class exists for this composite preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fish burger) is the closest defensible code. The dish uses battered dory fillet rather than a generic fish patty; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for battered dory burger.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 280 g, representing one complete battered dory fish fillet burger with tartar sauce and salad in a ciabatta bap or brioche bun.

nutrient_source.title: Fillet Me — Battered Dory Fish Burger (HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010985). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread and battered fish composite analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a battered dory fish burger. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate fraction: refined wheat bun (GI ~70–75, white bread analogue) and batter coating (GI ~60–65, battered fish analogue) weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution. Analogous foods drawn from University of Sydney GI Database entries for white bread (GI 75) and battered fish (GI ~58). Composite weighted estimate yields GI ~66. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (66 × 76.9) / 100 ≈ 50.8, rounded to 50.2 using 280 g serving × 27.48 g carb/100g = 76.9 g carb per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 571 mg/100g with low potassium-to-sodium ratio
hypertension.guidance: At 571 mg sodium per 100 g, a single 280 g serving delivers approximately 1,599 mg sodium — over 69% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — making this dish unsuitable for hypertensive individuals without significant sodium reduction; request preparation without added salt, limit tartar sauce, and consider a grilled rather than battered fillet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (27.5 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) and estimated medium-high GI (~66)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined bun and batter coating produce a high glycaemic load (~50 GL per serving), which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute the bun with a wholegrain alternative or lettuce wrap, request a grilled fillet, and limit or omit tartar sauce to reduce the overall glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation, elevated sodium, and cholesterol 21 mg/100g with unknown saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and likely elevates saturated and trans fat content beyond what is reported; combined with 571 mg/100g sodium, this dish poses a moderate-to-high cardiovascular risk and should be consumed only occasionally, with preference given to a grilled preparation and reduced-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generating AGEs; pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate coating; estimated trace trans fat from frying oil
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying of the dory fillet generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a grilled or baked fillet, avoid tartar sauce and mayonnaise, and consider substituting dory (low omega-3) with a fatty fish such as salmon or mackerel to gain anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fillet Me is a Western-style battered fish fillet burger served in Singapore fast-casual dining. The dish consists of a deep-fried battered dory (pangasius) fish fillet served in either a ciabatta bap or a brioche bun, accompanied by tartar sauce and a side salad. The batter coating is typically made from seasoned wheat flour, giving the fillet a crispy golden exterior while the dory flesh inside remains soft and mild-flavoured. Tartar sauce adds a creamy, tangy element. Per 100 g, the dish provides 281 kcal, 12.2 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and 27.5 g carbohydrate with only 1.7 g dietary fibre, reflecting a refined carbohydrate-dominant profile. Sodium is elevated at 571 mg/100g. A full 280 g serving delivers approximately 787 kcal and 1,599 mg sodium. The estimated glycaemic index is 66 (medium), yielding a high glycaemic load of approximately 50 per serving. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high refined carbohydrate, low fibre, medium-high GI), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried, high sodium, unknown saturated fat). It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation from deep-frying. The dish is halal-suitable and contains common allergens including gluten, fish, eggs, and dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fish fillet burger?
Is battered dory burger suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat a fish burger if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a fish burger?
Battered dory fish burger nutrition Singapore

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010985, confidence 0.95 in source record). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference foods and is documented as such. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB record.
notes: saturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo record for this dish. monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo record. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo record. trans_fat_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo record; estimated trace amount (~0.1 g/100g) noted in health suitability context from deep-frying but not confirmed by approved source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo record for this dish. This is a Western-style dish served in Singapore; while the cuisine is Western, as it is served in Singapore, mandatory local-language names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been added to ensure compliance with G6 for Singaporean dishes, as per the Project Veritas v2 guidelines. Halal status assessed as likely based on fish and vegetable ingredients; formal halal certification status of the specific outlet is not confirmed. Serving size of 280 g is an estimate for a complete assembled burger; actual weight may vary by outlet.
